Kara Bermejo

  • About

Building the foundation every feature now ships from

I initiated and led GrooverUI from zero to a shared, token-based design system — spanning web and mobile — for Groover's multilingual marketplace serving 50K+ artists and 3,500+ curators. It's now the foundation every feature ships from.

01 / Context

It had been tried before. It hadn't worked.

Before I joined, two previous attempts at a design system had stalled — once as an engineering-only effort with no design input, and once as a Figma library that never made it into production. Components were duplicated across repos, styles drifted between features, and mobile was an afterthought bolted onto desktop layouts.

It was clear that the problem wasn't talent or tooling — it was approach. A design system couldn't be a side project. It had to ship incrementally, tied to real feature work, with buy-in from both design and engineering from day one.

02 / Approach

Incremental by design — not all at once

Past attempts failed because they tried to systematize everything upfront. I took the opposite approach: ship components only when a feature needs them, then generalize.

  1. 1
    Start with what ships next.

    The first sprint of GrooverUI wasn't a component audit — it was the Spark sign-up redesign. Every token and component was born from a real shipping context, not a theoretical backlog.

  2. 2
    Formalize in Figma, validate in Storybook.

    Every component exists as a Figma variant and a Storybook story. Designers use the library; engineers reference the stories. Neither source drifts because both are part of the same PR review cycle.

  3. 3
    Expand only when friction appears.

    New patterns are added when the team encounters repeated inconsistency — not when someone imagines a future need. This kept the system lean and adoption high.

03 / The System

Tokens first. Components next. Patterns last.

GrooverUI is built in three layers. Tokens define the visual language — color, type, spacing. Components are the reusable building blocks. Patterns are the composed layouts that tie everything together for specific flows.

04 / Mobile-First Patterns

Designed for thumbs first.

Groover's audience is global and increasingly mobile. Every component and pattern is designed mobile-first — responsive type scales across three breakpoints, bottom sheets instead of modals on small screens, and touch targets that exceed accessibility minimums.

05 / Impact
20+

Components shipped to production across web and mobile

6

Core flows migrated to GrooverUI in the first two quarters

3

Breakpoints in the responsive type scale — mobile, tablet, desktop

06 / How It Evolved

From audit to OKR

Q3 2023

Joined Groover. Conducted full UI audit — catalogued 40+ inconsistent patterns across 12 flows.

Q4 2023

Shipped GrooverUI V1 alongside the Spark sign-up redesign. First 8 components in Figma + Storybook.

Q1 2024

Expanded to 15+ components. Migrated Track Form and Catalog flows. Responsive type scale finalized.

Q2 2024

Design system adopted as a formal OKR metric. Bottom sheet pattern shipped for all mobile flows.

2025

V2 expansion — FigmaMake + AI integration, 20+ components, dark mode token layer in progress.

07 / Reflections

What I'd tell the next person

Ship with features, not before them.

A design system that launches as a standalone initiative will stall. Tie every component to a real deadline and a real user flow.

Naming is half the work.

If designers and engineers can't agree on what to call something, they'll never agree on how to use it. Invest early in shared vocabulary.

Adoption beats coverage.

10 components used everywhere are worth more than 50 components used sometimes. Optimize for adoption, not completeness.
